narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, an LSAT score of 19.30, lower-middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3 both sitting at the 1st decile—meaning your early grades placed you at the bottom of your class compared to peers. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted an 80% chance that you would fail it on your first try, which directly led to the rejection of your application since the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. This high failure probability outweighed other factors like your solid undergraduate GPA, which is right around the typical 3.21 average for applicants. The three biggest influences pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction were, in order of strongest impact, your Year 3 decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 decile ranking—all of which pulled strongly in the direction of higher bar failure risk rather than success. Specifically, your low Year 3 decile, well below the average 5th or 6th decile for others, signaled to the model a concerning trend in your later academic performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Your LSAT score of 19.30, far under the typical 36.70 average, was the second-most powerful factor amplifying that risk, as stronger scores usually predict better bar outcomes. Even your Year 1 decile at the bottom, lower than the average around the 5th or 6th, added to this pattern, though with slightly less weight. Compared to typical students who get approved—those with mid-to-high deciles, LSATs in the 30s or above, and balanced profiles—your early and sustained low class rankings combined with the low LSAT stood out as red flags that deviated sharply from successful applicants. Your full-time status aligns with most (over 90%) and didn't sway things much, nor did your GPA or family background in a major way here. While this rejection stings, especially given your determination as an Asian male navigating competitive paths, it highlights clear areas for growth. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, seeking academic support to improve your decile rankings in any further studies, and building a stronger narrative in re-applications that addresses these gaps—perhaps through targeted prep courses or additional credentials. Many students in similar spots have turned things around with focused effort, and reapplying after strengthening these key areas could change the model's view and open doors. Keep pushing; your story isn't over yet.
